prompt: At the most basic level, what does a large language model do when it generates a response?
choices: Looks each answer up in a connected fact database
Predicts the next token repeatedly, one token at a time
Executes the user's prompt as program code
Searches the live internet for the best matching page

prompt: Which of the following are genuine limitations you must design around when using an LLM? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Hallucination — it can state false information confidently
Knowledge cutoff — it lacks data after its training date
Non-determinism — the same prompt may yield different answers
It cannot produce text longer than one sentence

prompt: What is the difference between training and inference?
choices: Training happens on every request; inference happens once
Training learns the model's weights once; inference uses those frozen weights to answer each prompt
They are two names for the same process
Inference updates the model's knowledge with each new question

prompt: A PM is scoping a high-volume feature that classifies short messages into 4 fixed categories, and must keep cost low and outputs repeatable. Which choice best fits?
choices: The largest available model at high temperature for creativity
The smallest model that reliably does the task, at low temperature
Any model at high temperature, since classification needs variety
A model with the most recent knowledge cutoff, regardless of size
